coredata.dc:title: Intracellular diatom-derived carbon dots for enhancing photosynthetic efficiency and biomass production of Chlorella
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaoqi
Zheng, Yimeng
Zhang, Yuanshuai
Chi, Zirong
Zeng, Yanhua
Huang, Aiyou
Xie, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description: Optimizing photosynthesis in microalgae is crucial for enhancing bioenergy production and addressing global energy demands. Here, the diatom-derived carbon dots (D-CDs) were synthesized through a hydrothermal method and explored their impact on the growth and photosynthesis of Chlorella. The results demonstrated that D-CDs significantly improved Chlorella growth, with a 59.4% increase in biomass, a 35.3% increase in proteins and a 118.9% increase in carbohydrates. The D-CDs penetrated the cells and interacted with chloroplasts, accelerating photosynthetic electron transfer during light-dependent reactions. This led to a 26.3% increase in ATP and a 55.5% increase in NADPH production. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that D-CDs upregulated ribosome-related genes and stimulated the expression of genes encoding nitrite reductase and glutamine synthetase, thereby enhancing nitrogen assimilation and utilization. This study highlights the potential of CDs derived from biomass in promoting Chlorella growth and enhancing photosynthesis, offering a promising approach for sustainable bioresource development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microalgae
Quantum dot
Growth improvement
Photosynthesis
Transcriptome

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The diatom-derived carbon dots assembly with Chlorella
The mechanisms of diatom-derived carbon dots driving photosynthesis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the 2024 Marine biomedicine field of provincial science and technology plan directed project (2024Y0079), Hainan Agricultural Production Research System (HNARS-10-ZJ05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)The preparation of D-CDs. (B) Photographs of D-CDs under daylight and 420 nm. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra, and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of D-CDs in ultrapure water. (D) HRTEM images of D-CDs with the inset showing size distribution histograms.

The CLSM images of Chlorella (A) and D-CDs-treated Chlorella (B). (C) TEM images showing the ultrastructure of Chlorella cells exposed to different concentrations of D-CDs after 24 h (CW, cell wall; C, chloroplast; T, thylakoids; P, pyrenoid; N, nucleus; V, vacuole; L, lipid droplet; M, mitochondrion; S, starch grain).

(A) Growth profiles of Chlorella incubated with different concentrations of D-CDs. Effect of D-CDs on (B, C, and D) pigment. (E) Lipid, protein and carbohydrate. (F) The measurement of light curve. (G) Maximum photosynthetic efficiency (Fv/Fm) and electron transport rate (ETRmax). (H) Ik and α. (I)NADPH, NADP+ and ATP levels in Chlorella with and without D-CDs treatment. (n = 3 technical replicates, bars represent the mean ± sd.)

Differentially expressed genes in control group (C1, C2 and C3) and D-CDs treatment group (T1, T2 and T3). (A) Clustering analysis. (B) Volcano map. (C) GO function classification. (D) EggNOG classification. (E) KEGG pathway analysis.

Schematic representation of transcriptional expressions of genes involved in ribosome, nitrogen metabolism, cysteine and methionine metabolism, nucleotide metabolism and ribosome biogenesis in eukaryotes. (glnA: glutamine synthetase; ahcy: adenosylhomocysteinase; nirA: nitrite reductase; glu: glutamate synthase; yhde: Dehydroquinase; CSNK2B: casein kinase II subunit beta.)

head.ce:title: Intracellular diatom-derived carbon dots for enhancing photosynthetic efficiency and biomass production of Chlorella

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Health Sciences, Hainan University, Haikou 570228 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life and Health Sciences, Hainan University, Haikou 570228 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Marine Biology and Fisheries, Hainan University, Haikou 570228 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Marine Biology and Fisheries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Marine Biology and Fisheries, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Optimizing photosynthesis in microalgae is crucial for enhancing bioenergy production and addressing global energy demands. Here, the diatom-derived carbon dots (D-CDs) were synthesized through a hydrothermal method and explored their impact on the growth and photosynthesis of Chlorella. The results demonstrated that D-CDs significantly improved Chlorella growth, with a 59.4% increase in biomass, a 35.3% increase in proteins and a 118.9% increase in carbohydrates. The D-CDs penetrated the cells and interacted with chloroplasts, accelerating photosynthetic electron transfer during light-dependent reactions. This led to a 26.3% increase in ATP and a 55.5% increase in NADPH production. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that D-CDs upregulated ribosome-related genes and stimulated the expression of genes encoding nitrite reductase and glutamine synthetase, thereby enhancing nitrogen assimilation and utilization. This study highlights the potential of CDs derived from biomass in promoting Chlorella growth and enhancing photosynthesis, offering a promising approach for sustainable bioresource development.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthesis is a fundamental biological process in photosynthetic organisms, converting solar energy into chemical energy to support life activities and sustain ecosystems on Earth (Zhou et al., 2020). Microalgae have garnered significant attention because they account for about 45 % of the total photosynthetic organisms (Huang et al., 2023). They are considered a promising sustainable bioenergy source, offering a potential alternative to fossil fuels because of their high growth rates, rich lipid and carbohydrate content (Milano et al., 2016). However, the low solar utilization efficiency of the photosynthesis system remains a significant barrier to improving microalgae yields and nutrient accumulation (Xue et al., 2020). Enhancing the efficiency of photosynthesis in microalgae has therefore become a critical focus, addressing the growing global energy demands and environmental challenges (Nowicka et al., 2018).

Integrating artificial semiconductors with photosynthetic systems opens new avenues to enhance microalgae photosynthetic efficiency and overall productivity (Lau et al., 2022). Recent studies have explored various semiconductors that interact with microalgae to enhance photosynthetic activity, including carbon-based nanomaterials, metal oxide-based nanomaterials, and noble metal-based nanomaterial (Middepogu et al., 2018, Cong et al., 2024). Although metal oxide-based and noble metal-based nanomaterials positively impact photosynthesis, there are still some limitations in the process of practical applications. These include high material costs and ecological risks due to the accumulation in aquatic organisms (Agathokleous et al., 2019) or the release of toxic metal ions (Das et al., 2017). Thus, carbon-based nanomaterials have garnered significant attention in photosynthesis research due to their relative affordability and lower environmental impact.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, have emerged as promising candidates because of their distinctive optical properties, biocompatibility, low toxicity and high-water solubility (Hu et al., 2022). The selection of appropriate carbon sources is critical in synthesizing CDs, as it directly influences their performance, sustainability, and cost (Zhu et al., 2020). Biomass, recognized for its heteroatom abundance, low cost, and sustainability, is an ideal candidate for CDs preparation (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). The use of biomass as a precursor not only addresses cost concerns but also enhances the environmental sustainability of the CDs synthesis process (Fang et al., 2024). Biomass-derived CDs have been employed to enhance crop photosynthesis, leveraging their excellent fluorescence properties and potential as electron donors. For instance, CDs derived from soybeans have been shown to improve the photosynthetic efficiency of Brassica chinensis L. leaves by broadening the spectrum of absorbed light (Cheng et al., 2023). Similarly, functionalized CDs from Exocarpium Citri Grandis have significantly increased the photosynthetic efficiency and electron transport rate in lettuce leaves (Cen et al., 2023). Additionally, CDs derived from Phellodendron chinense Schneid exhibit strong absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) light region and emit intense blue and red light, leading to the production of 2.8 times more adenosine triphosphate (ATP) than chloroplasts alone in vitro (Li et al., 2018). Although several potential pathways for biomass-derived CDs in enhancing crop photosynthetic activity have been proposed, the mechanisms underlying their effects on improving microalgal photosynthetic efficiency remain largely unexplored. Consequently, there is an urgent need to investigate the role of biomass-derived CDs in microalgae photosynthesis.

In this study, CDs derived from Phaeodactylum tricornutum, a diatom species known for its natural abundance and sustainable harvest potential, were synthesized using a solvothermal synthesis method. The application of these diatom-derived CDs (D-CDs) in enhancing the photosynthetic efficiency of Chlorella was subsequently explored. D-CDs were comprehensively characterized, focusing on their effects on Chlorella biomass and nutritional content, including intracellular proteins, carbohydrates, and lipids. To elucidate the mechanisms by which D-CDs enhance nutritional content and biomass, the mode of action of D-CDs and Chlorella was investigated. Specifically, D-CDs penetrate Chlorella cells and interact with chloroplasts, accelerating photosynthetic electron transfer during light-dependent reactions. Additionally, transcriptome sequencing and analysis further revealed that D-CDs upregulate ribosome-related genes and stimulate the expression of genes encoding nitrite reductase and glutamine synthetase, thereby enhancing nitrogen assimilation and utilization. By deepening the understanding of how D-CDs impact photosynthetic processes and biomass production in microalgae, this research contributes to developing sustainable and efficient bioenergy solutions.

CDs were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal approach. Specifically, the Phaeodactylum tricornutum powder was dispersed in 60 mL of a mixed water/ethanol solvent (volume ratio of 2:3). This solution was sonicated and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to a reaction at 160 °C for 6 h. Following the reaction, the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally and was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to eliminate the precipitate. The supernatant was dialyzed (MWCO 500) for 24 h and then freeze-dried to obtain solid CDs.

The morphology and particle size of the CDs were measured using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Talos F200X G2). The chemical structure and composition of CDs were determined using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, PANalytical X'Pert3) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi), respectively. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were obtained on an FS5 Spectrofluorometer from Edinburgh instruments. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a Horiba Fluorolog-3. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an FTIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Frontier).

Chlorella cells were cultured in BG-11 medium and maintained in a controlled environment incubator at 25 °C, with a light intensity of 86 μmol/m2/s and a 12/12 h light/dark cycle. The conical flasks were artificially shaken three times daily. The Chlorella was diluted to an initial optical density (OD680) of 0.1 at 680 nm to establish the starting cell concentration for growth curve analysis. Subsequently, the prepared carbon dot solutions were added to the cell suspension and the final D-CDs concentrations were 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mg/L. Each treatment was conducted in triplicate. Samples were collected every two days, thorough mixing of the algal suspension before sampling, and the OD680 values were recorded. The ΔOD680 was determined by subtracting the initial OD680 (OD0) from the OD680 at the time of measurement (ODt).

The chlorophyll a, b and carotenoid contents were determined following a previously reported method (Yang et al., 2022). Specifically, 5 mL microalgae suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C to remove supernatant. The precipitation was then resuspended in 5 mL of 95 % methanol and incubated at 4°C in the dark for 24 h. After that, the suspension was centrifuged again at 10000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was measured at 663 nm, 645 nm and 470 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Chlorophyll fluorescence of the microalgae was assessed using a Water-PAM Chlorophyll Fluorometer (Walz GmbH, Effeltrich, Germany). The cell solution was dark-adapted for 15 min before measurement and then the maximum photosynthetic efficiency (Fv/Fm) was measured to evaluate the activity of PSII. Light response curves were utilized to fit the relative electron transport rate versus irradiance. Specifically, a 2 mL sample of microalgae culture was exposed to ten different actinic light levels (0, 36, 81, 146, 231, 396, 461, 611, 801, 1076 and1251 μmol/m2/s). The photosynthetic parameters α, ETRmax, and Ik were obtained through light curve fitting by WinControl-3 software. The parameter α described the initial slope of the rapid light curve, reflecting the light energy utilization efficiency; ETRmax indicated the maximum electron transport rate; Ik represented the ability to tolerate strong light.

The protein content was quantified using a bicinchoninic acid (BCA) protein assay kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, Nanjing, China), with bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the reference standard. Briefly, 3 mL of 10 mM PBS buffer (pH = 7.2) was dropped into 10 mg freeze-dried algae powder, followed by 20 min of sonication in an ice bath. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was collected for further analysis. The absorbance of the supernatant was then measured at 562 nm using the BCA assay kit.

The lipid content of the microalgae was measured using the chloroform–methanol extraction method. Briefly, 20 mg of freeze-dried algae powder was mixed with 1 mL methanol-chloroform mixture (1:1 v/v) and stirred vigorously for 5 min. Following this, 0.3 mL H3PO4 (0.2 M) containing 1 M KCl was added to the solution. The solvent phase was collected by centrifuging the mixture for 5 min at 5000 rpm. The above extraction sequence was repeated three times to collect all chloroform layers, which were then washed with 1 mL of distilled water. Finally, the solvent was evaporated in a fume hood, and the remaining lipid was weighed to determine the total lipid content.

The total carbohydrate content was quantified using the anthrone sulfuric acid method (Xue et al., 2020). Initially, 10 mg freeze-dried algae powder was mixed with 3 mL of 80 % ethanol and incubated at 68°C for 15 min. After incubation, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm to isolate the supernatant. The remaining pellet was then dissolved in 1.5 mL of 30 % perchloric acid for another 15 min, followed by a second centrifugation to collect the supernatant. The two supernatants were subsequently merged to create the extraction solution. Next, 5 mL of anthrone-sulfuric acid reagent, made by dissolving 0.1 g of anthrone in 100 mL of 80 % concentrated sulfuric acid, was introduced to 1 mL of the extraction solution. The resulting mixture was boiled at 100 °C for 10 min. After allowing it to cool to room temperature, the absorbance was measured at 620 nm to quantify the carbohydrate concentration.

Chlorella and Chlorella/CDs suspensions were first centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min to obtain a pellet. After that, the pellet was used for ATP, NADP+, and NADPH content measured by an ATP assay kit and NADP+/NADPH assay kits.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi-7800) was used to analyze microalgae cells’ ultrastructure. The interaction between CDs and microalgal cells was investigated through confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Nikon A1R HD25).

The transcriptome sequencing of Chlorella used in this experiment was conducted by Shanghai Majorbio Bio-pharm Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The raw sequencing data have been deposited into the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) with the BioProject number of PRJNA1205531. RNA extraction was performed on collected algal cells, followed by purification and reverse transcription to construct high-quality cDNA libraries. Raw reads obtained through sequencing were filtered using Fastp software to produce clean reads. Trinity was utilized for de novo assembly of all clean data, and the resulting assemblies were evaluated to obtain reference sequences for subsequent analysis. The sequences were aligned with those in the NR, Swiss-Prot, Pfam, COG, KO, and KEGG databases for annotation. Gene expression levels were quantified using RSEM, and DESeq2 was employed to detect differentially expressed genes (DEGs), with |log2FoldChange|>1 and P-adjust < 0.05 considered significant. The DEGs were subjected to functional enrichment analysis using the GO and KEGG databases, allowing for the inference of their primary functions and biological significance.

Each test was measured in triplicate, and the results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical significance was determined by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) or independent t-tests, with analyses performed using SPSS 23.0 software. A P-value of less than 0.05 was regarded as statistically significant.

Under light irradiation at a wavelength of 420 nm (10 W), the solution of diatom-derived carbon dots (D-CDs) emits a visually striking orange-red fluorescence (Fig. 1 B). As shown in Fig. 1C, the black line in the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs reveals an absorption band that peaks at 410 nm, which is typically attributed to the Soret band of porphyrins from chlorophyll (Li et al., 2018). In Fig. 1C (blue line), the excitation spectrum shows an intense peak at 410 nm when the D-CDs are monitored at 676 nm (optimal emission). In addition, the D-CDs display multiple emissions with wavelengths ranging from 450 to 800 nm under 410 nm excitation (the red line of Fig. 1C). Additionally, the absolute quantum yield of D-CDs was measured as 5.2 % using a steady state and time-resolved photoluminescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Noteworthy, the emission peak of the D-CDs at 676 nm remains consistent regardless of the excitation wavelength (λex). However, the fluorescence intensity rises within the λex range of 370 to 410 nm, subsequently decreasing as the λex increases from 410 nm to 470 nm (see supplementary material). This behavior aligns with the luminescence characteristics observed in previously reported porphyrin derivatives (Liang et al., 2023). Furthermore, D-CDs exhibit good stability across a pH range of 2–12, various NaCl concentrations (0.1–2 M), and a temperature range of 10–70 °C, respectively (see supplementary material).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images revealed that the D-CDs are spherical and exhibit good monodisperse in aqueous solutions (Fig. 1D). The size distribution analysis indicates that the average particle size of the D-CDs is 5.6 ± 1.2 nm. However, there are no lattice fringes were observed from the HRTEM image, indicating the prepared D-CDs are non-crystalline structures. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) results corroborate this finding, displaying a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.5° (see supplementary material), which signifies non-crystalline structures characterized by highly disordered carbon atoms.

The surface functional groups of D-CDs were analyzed utilizing Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (see supplementary material). The broad absorption band observed between 3200–3700 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups (Li et al., 2021). The absorption peaks at 2960, 2924, and 2851 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CH bonds and the absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC bonds (Agnol et al., 2021). Peaks located at 1160 and 1250 cm−1 are indicative of CN and CO bonds, while the peak at 1048 cm−1 is associated with the COC bond (Zhang et al., 2023). Additionally, the peak at 1418 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the CN bond. The FTIR results demonstrate that the D-CDs possess various hydrophilic functional groups, including amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which contribute to their enhanced water solubility.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used for the investigation of elemental composition and surface valence state (see supplementary material). High-resolution C 1s spectra revealed peaks corresponding to different carbon bonds: CO/C-OH at 287.3 eV, CC at 284.8 eV, and CC at 283.8 eV (Wang et al., 2023). The high-resolution N 1s spectra showed peaks at 400.0 eV, 399.3 eV, and 398.6 eV, attributed to graphitic N (N-C3), pyrrolic N (C2 NH), and pyridinic N (CNC), respectively (Huang et al., 2023). Similarly, the high-resolution O 1s spectra displayed peaks for CO and CO bonds at 530.8 eV and 532.1 eV, respectively (Xue et al., 2023). The functional groups identified by XPS analysis were consistent with those observed in FTIR spectra, confirming the presence and bonding states of groups in the D-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The diatom-derived carbon dots assembly with Chlorella
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the interaction between D-CDs and Chlorella, the zeta potential was first measured and confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was then used for further evaluation. Both D-CDs and Chlorella exhibited negatively charged surfaces in BG-11 medium (see supplementary material), indicating that D-CDs cannot bond to the Chlorella surface through electrostatic interactions. Since electrostatic bonding was ruled out, CLSM was employed to explore other possible interaction mechanisms. The control group without D-CDs only showed fluorescence from chloroplasts at 663–738 nm (Fig. 2 A), whereas Chlorella incubated with D-CDs exhibited additional green fluorescence at 500–550 nm (Fig. 2B), revealing that D-CDs had penetrated the Chlorella cells. Importantly, compared to the control group, Chlorella cells incubated with 20 and 50 mg/L of D-CDs exhibited no significant changes in morphology (Fig. 2C). The ultrastructure of chloroplasts, vacuoles, and starch granules remained largely intact, with no noticeable differences. However, at higher concentrations, such as 100 mg/L, D-CDs induced cellular deformation and caused blurred chloroplast lamellae. These findings suggest that low concentrations of D-CDs are non-toxic to Chlorella.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanisms of diatom-derived carbon dots driving photosynthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the influence of D-CDs on Chlorella growth, varying concentrations of D-CDs were systematically tested, and the responses of algae were monitored. The results demonstrated that the addition of D-CDs improved Chlorella growth in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 3 A). Chlorella exhibited maximum growth, achieving a peak growth rate of 59.4 % at a concentration of 50 mg/L. However, the growth rate decreased at a D-CDs concentration of 100 mg/L, which may likely inhibit photosynthesis and growth caused by excessive production of reactive oxygen species (see supplementary material). Consequently, the 50 mg/L D-CDs were selected as the optimal concentration for subsequent experiments.

Given that Chlorella growth is closely linked to photosynthesis, the effect of D-CDs on chlorophyll content was further explored. On the 6th day of culture, a significant increase in pigment content was observed, particularly when the D-CDs concentration was 50 mg/L. After 12 days of culture, Chlorella treated with 50 mg/L D-CDs had chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b representing increases of 56.9 % and 41.8 % (Fig. 3B-D). the pigment content to biomass was normalized to more accurately compare changes in pigment content due to D-CDs. The results indicated that compared to the control group, pigment content significantly increased on the 6th day when the D-CD concentration was below 100 mg/L (P < 0.05, see supplementary material). The results reveal that D-CDs can promote the synthesis of more chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b in Chlorella, thereby enhancing light energy absorption and transfer, improving the efficiency of the photosynthetic system reaction centers, and consequently promoting cell growth.

To understand how D-CDs affect the intracellular components of Chlorella, the soluble protein, carbohydrate, and lipid contents were measured with results illustrated in Fig. 3E. The measurements of protein, carbohydrate, lipid and contents in Chlorella were initially found to be 9.9 %, 3.7 %, and 20.1 %, respectively. Post-treatment with D-CDs, the protein and carbohydrate contents increased significantly to 13.4 % and 8.1 %, reflecting enhancement rates of 35.3 % and 118.9 %, respectively. Conversely, the lipid content remained relatively stable at 18.2 %, showing no notable variation from the control group. The results regarding intracellular components indicated that microalgae preferentially utilized fixed carbon to synthesize intracellular protein and carbohydrates. Moreover, the absence of a significant difference in lipid content may be attributed to the microalgae culture having not yet reached a stage of nutritional starvation, thereby preventing the accumulation of intracellular lipids (Chisti, 2007).

The photosynthetic activity of Chlorella was investigated by measuring several key parameters: Fv/Fm, ETRmax, α, and Ik. As shown in Fig. 3F, the electron transport rate (ETR) increases with photosynthetically active radiation (PAR), with Chlorella/D-CDs consistently showing higher values. The maximum electron transport rate (ETRmax) increased from 51.2 to 59.9 μmol·m−2·s−1. This enhancement demonstrates that D-CDs can facilitate electron transfer within the photosynthetic electron transport chain. D-CDs likely provide additional electrons, which enhance the efficiency of electron transfer from Photosystem II (PSII) to Photosystem I (PSI). This increased electron flow supports multiple anabolic processes during photosynthesis, thereby boosting overall photosynthetic efficiency (Zhou et al., 2020). The Fv/Fm value was enhanced by 21.3 % following treatment with D-CDs (Fig. 3G). This significant increase indicates enhanced photosynthetic activity, as more light energy is transformed into chemical energy in existing D-CDs. Additionally, the changes in the fluorescence lifetime of D-CDs, both before and following their assembly with Chlorella, confirmed this result. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs at 676 nm decreased from 4.9 nm to 4.2 nm after forming the Chlorella/D-CDs complex while there was no significant change at 635 nm (see supplementary material). The phenomenon can be attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between D-CDs and Chlorella, suggesting the energy from D-CDs can be taken up and utilized by Chlorella when exposed to light.

The initial slope α of the rapid light curve reflects the light energy utilization efficiency. Fitting the rapid light curve indicated that the presence of D-CDs improves the light capture efficiency of Chlorella (Fig. 3H). Despite this improvement, there was no significant change in the light resistance intensity (Ik). This suggests that D-CDs enhance the initial stages of light capture but do not influence Chlorella's ability to tolerate high light intensities. D-CDs also significantly increased the content of NADPH and NADP+ (Fig. 3I), which are crucial for the Calvin cycle and other anabolic processes. Specifically, NADPH and NADP+ content increased by 55.5 % and 33.9 %. These increases suggest that D-CDs facilitate the conversion processes between NADPH and NADP+, confirming enhanced electron transfer from PSII to PSI. Furthermore, the ATP content exhibited a significant increase of 26.3 %. This suggests that the enhanced electron transfer rate has intensified the proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane, thereby facilitating increased ATP synthesis (Feng et al., 2016). Following the light reaction, CO2 is converted into carbohydrates via the Calvin cycle, with ATP produced during this process supplying essential energy, which directly influences biomass accumulation.

To reveal the molecular mechanism by which D-CDs enhance Chlorella growth, transcriptome analysis on both control and experimental groups was conducted. Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between groups were identified, yielding a total of 41.6 to 44.9 million clean reads. The Q30 range of raw reads for each group was between 95.2 % and 95.6 %, with Q20 values above 98.4 % (see supplementary material). These data indicate high sequencing quality, sufficient for subsequent analysis. Fig. 4 A shows a heat map of the clustered DEGs, visually depicting the dynamic changes in gene expression, where blue represents low-expression genes and red represents high-expression genes. The volcano plot of the DEGs shows a total of 476 DEGs were identified, with 407 genes upregulated and 69 genes downregulated following incubation with D-CDs (Fig. 4B). These results suggest that the addition of D-CDs to the medium altered the gene expression profile of Chlorella, particularly by upregulating specific genes. This alteration may account for the enhanced growth and metabolism of Chlorella.

Subsequently, the KEGG, EggNOG and GO functional pathways for DEGS were analyzed. As shown in GO functional analysis, there are 22 secondary GO terms were involved in the DEGs in the three main categories: biological processes, cellular components, and molecular functions (Fig. 4C). In the cellular component category, “cell part”, “membrane” and “organelle” were dominant. Within biological processes, “cellular process” and “metabolic process” were predominant. In the molecular function category, “binding” and “catalytic activity” were most representative. EggNOG (Fig. 4D) functional pathway analysis showed that most DEGs were enriched in amino acid transport and metabolism (E), translation, ribosomal structure and biogenesis (J), signal transduction mechanisms (T), and post-translational modification, protein turnover, chaperones (O) These findings indicate that the identified DEGs are closely associated with basic metabolic activities and structural functions of the cell. DEGs significantly enriched in KEGG pathways are related to “translation”, “carbohydrate metabolism”, “amino acid metabolism” and “energy metabolism”(Fig. 4E). The above results suggest that the DEGs are most associated with fundamental cellular metabolic processes, protein synthesis and structural functions.

The KEGG enrichment analysis demonstrates the pathways of “ribosome” and “nitrogen metabolism” are significant enrichment (see supplementary material). Notably, transcriptional expressions of genes showing genes encoding ribosomal proteins (rps11, rpsa, rpl4, rpl23, rpl18, rps5) were significantly upregulated (Fig. 5 ). It is important to note that ribosomes are primarily responsible for translating RNA into proteins, playing a crucial role in the growth, and development of eukaryotic cells and organisms (Zhu et al., 2019). Thus, the upregulated genes encoding ribosomal proteins can further improve the growth and proliferation of Chlorella cells.

Nitrogen is one of the essential nutrients for cell growth. It can be converted into chlorophyll in chloroplasts, thereby increasing pigment content and photosynthesis, or into amino acids for other metabolic pathways (Zhang et al., 2022). Moreover, nitrite reductase plays a crucial role in enhancing the nitrogen assimilation efficiency of microalgae by converting nitrite into ammonia. The upregulated genes encoding nitrite reductase (nirA) may enhance nitrate assimilation, allowing microalgal cells to absorb more nitrogen and support their rapid growth (Fig. 5). Similarly, D-CDs enhance nitrogen metabolism and amino acid biosynthesis by promoting photosynthesis and increasing nitrate reductase and nitrite reductase activities as also demonstrated in Ji et al. study (Ji et al., 2023). Glutamine synthetase, another crucial enzyme in the nitrogen assimilation of eukaryotic algae, serves as the primary nitrogen donor for the biosynthesis of various organic nitrogen compounds, including purines, pyrimidines, and other amino acids. It is involved in multiple biosynthesis pathways, providing essential energy, carbon, and nitrogen skeletons, ultimately promoting biomass accumulation (Yutthanasirikul et al., 2024). The upregulation of its encoded gene (glnA) transcription level suggests an enhanced process of ammonia assimilation into glutamine, increasing nitrogen assimilation efficiency and supporting protein synthesis and cell growth (Fig. 5). Similarly, Tan et al. also observed that carbon dots positively influence plant nitrogen metabolism by promoting glutamine synthetase activity (Tan et al., 2023).

Besides analyzing metabolic pathways, genes related to cell proliferation were also analyzed. As shown in Fig. 5, the genes CSNK2B, yhdE, and ahcy, encoding casein kinase II β subunit, dehydroquinase and adenosylhomocysteinase respectively, are upregulated following treatment with D-CDs. β subunit of casein kinase II is involved in cell cycle control, DNA repair and signal transduction (Roustan et al., 2017). Upregulation of its encoding gene (CSNK2B) may enhance Chlorella growth by promoting DNA repair and cell proliferation since Chlorella needs a stable cell cycle and efficient DNA repair mechanisms for effective cell division (Yang et al., 2023). This suggests that D-CDs may support cell stability and growth by strengthening DNA repair mechanisms and regulating the cell cycle. Additionally, Dehydroquinase (encoded by yhdE) plays a crucial role in the aromatic amino acid synthesis pathway, catalyzing the conversion of dehydroquinic acid to dehydroshikimic acid. Dehydroshikimic acid is a precursor to both the hormone abscisic acid (ABA) and protocatechuic acid, which can promote green algae growth according to a previous report (Del Mondo et al., 2022). Thus, the upregulated yhdE level may have a positive effect on Chlorella growth. Moreover, Adenosylhomocysteinase (encoded by ahcy), an important enzyme in the methylation cycle, can catalyze the degradation of S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH) to generate homocysteine. Upregulation of ahcy stimulates the SAM cycle and methylation processes, playing a role in various cellular functions such as transcription, translation, DNA stabilization, and cell growth. It also contributes to protein, DNA, and RNA biosynthesis (Yan et al., 2023).

In summary, D-CDs derived from diatom were successfully synthesized and shown to enhance the photosynthetic efficiency and biomass production of Chlorella significantly. It was found that D-CDs can enter algal cells and significantly promote Chlorella growth and metabolism through multiple mechanisms. By increasing chlorophyll content and enhancing photosynthetic efficiency, D-CDs improve light absorption and electron transfer, thereby accelerating NADPH and ATP production and promoting biomass accumulation. Additionally, D-CDs significantly enhance the synthesis of proteins and carbohydrates. Transcriptomic analysis reveals that D-CDs modulate the expression of genes related to ribosome function, nitrogen metabolism, protein synthesis, and DNA repair, further contributing to growth and metabolic enhancement. This work expands the understanding of D-CD applications in microalgae and introduces innovative strategies for advancing bioenergy production while optimizing the use of biological resources in microalgal systems.

Xiaoqi Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Yimeng Zheng: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Yuanshuai Zhang: Data curation. Zirong Chi: Methodology. Yanhua Zeng: Formal analysis. Aiyou Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenyu Xie: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the 2024 Marine biomedicine field of provincial science and technology plan directed project (2024Y0079), Hainan Agricultural Production Research System (HNARS-10-ZJ05).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2025.132129.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
